title: The Gaelic Athlete, vol. VIII, no. 192, Saturday September 4th 1915

pages.text:
September 4, 1915

Ireland's Record in Athletic Sports

From the Tailtean Games to the Present Time

Including the Complete History of the Rise and Development of the G.A.A.

By "VIGILANT."

XXX - A FIERCE BLIZZARD

The Freeman's Journal now continued to make trouble for the Association in general, and the Executive in particular with increasing zeal. The GAA, which had been the only one in the country to have a handicapped over the question of the handicap, was entirely defeated by the Freeman's Freeman's Journal Athletic Club and its in- sistance on its "right" to ignore a rule of the Association that had been confirmed at the Thirtieth Congress of the G.A.A. was regarded as a defeat for the G.A.A., and made some scathing references to the Executive's power of suspension. This, however, was only a small step in the direction of the Freeman's Journal Athletic Club, and left nothing undone to discredit the Executive. The committee in charge of the clause of the G.A.A. had taken a different view of the situation, and upheld the rules of the Association and the decisions of the Executive by debarring sus- pended athletes from the G.A.A. The Freeman's Journal looked forward to the pleasure of giving the G.A.A. its final fall in its own sports meeting, and left nothing undone to discredit the Executive. The committee in charge of the clause of the G.A.A. had taken a different view of the situation, and upheld the rules of the Association and the decisions of the Executive by debarring sus- pended athletes from the G.A.A.
